conditions: The magnitude depends on stem inclusion percentage and the potassium content of the specific vineyard's stems. Effect is less pronounced in smaller proportions (10–20% stems) and significant at 50–100% whole bunch. Can be undesirable in already low-acid warm-climate grapes where pH may rise to microbiologically risky levels (>3.7–3.8).
explanation: Grape stems contain high concentrations of potassium ions (K⁺). During fermentation, ethanol and mechanical action (pump-overs, punch-downs) extract potassium from stem tissue into the must. Potassium then precipitates tartaric acid as potassium bitartrate (cream of tartar), removing free tartaric acid from solution, raising pH, and reducing total titratable acidity. This is a pH-raising mechanism specific to stem inclusion and distinct from the flavour/tannin effects of whole bunch.
